body.text: This note is written as a source document for formalization. All groups are written multiplicatively unless an abelian group is explicitly written additively.

body.label: Definition 1
body.definition: Let G be a group with identity element e. A function l : G -> R is a pseudo-length function on G if the following four conditions hold: nonnegativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality.
body.name: PseudoLength

body.text: The nonnegativity condition is included explicitly so that the codomain can be the usual real numbers in a formalization. Equivalently, one may take the codomain to be the set of nonnegative real numbers and omit the separate nonnegativity axiom.

body.label: Definition 2
body.definition: Let G be a group with identity element e. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is a length function if, for every g in G, g != e implies 0 < l(g).
body.name: IsLength

body.label: Definition 3
body.definition: Let G be a group. A pseudo-length function l : G -> R is homogeneous if, for every g in G and every integer n in Z, l(g^n) = |n| l(g). In particular, for every g in G, homogeneity gives l(g^2)=2l(g).
body.name: IsHomogeneousPseudoLength

body.label: Definition 4
body.definition: Let G be a group. For elements x,y in G, define the commutator [x,y] := xyx^{-1}y^{-1}.
body.name: commutator

body.label: Definition 5
body.definition: Let G be a group. The commutator subgroup [G,G] is the subgroup of G generated by the set {[x,y] | x,y in G}.
body.name: commutatorSubgroup

body.label: Definition 6
body.definition: Let G be a group. The abelianization of G is the quotient group G_ab := G/[G,G]. Let pi_ab : G -> G_ab denote the quotient homomorphism.
body.name: abelianization

body.label: Definition 7
body.definition: Let A be an abelian group, written additively. The torsion subgroup T(A) is the subgroup T(A) := {a in A | exists n in Z, n != 0 and n a = 0}.
body.name: torsionSubgroup

body.label: Definition 8
body.definition: An abelian group A is torsion-free if T(A)={0}.
body.name: TorsionFree

proof.text: Fix x,y in G. First prove l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x). Let n be a positive integer. By homogeneity, n l(yxy^{-1}) = l((yxy^{-1})^n). Since (yxy^{-1})^n = yx^n y^{-1}, triangle inequality, symmetry, and homogeneity give n l(yxy^{-1}) <= 2l(y)+n l(x). Dividing by n gives l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x)+2l(y)/n. Letting n tend to infinity yields l(yxy^{-1}) <= l(x). For the reverse inequality, define z := yxy^{-1}. Then x=y^{-1}zy. Applying the proved inequality with z and y^{-1} gives l(x) <= l(z)=l(yxy^{-1}). Hence equality holds.

proof.text: Fix x,y in G and define c:=[x,y]. Let n be a positive integer. By homogeneity, n l(c)=l(c^n)=f(0,n), where f(m,k):=l(x^m c^k). Lemma 5 gives f(0,n) <= E[f(S_{2n},-S_{2n}/2)]. deduced_from_claim: A Hamel basis of V has been chosen

deduced_from_theorem.claim: Every vector in a vector space has a unique finite linear combination representation with respect to a basis.
deduced_from_theorem.name: Hamel basis coordinate representation
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to the chosen Hamel basis of V to obtain unique finite support coordinates for each v in V.

proof_steps.claim: Each v in V has unique finite support coordinates in the chosen Hamel basis.
proof_steps.proof_method: By the defining property of the chosen Hamel basis.

proof_steps.text: Every v in V has a unique finite coordinate expansion with respect to the chosen Hamel basis.

proof_steps.label: homogeneous.root.cor15.proof.root.mpr.basis_norm.norm_properties
proof_steps.claim: basisNorm is a norm on V.

deduced_from_claim: basisNorm(v) is defined as the l1 norm of the finite coordinate coefficients of v with respect to the chosen Hamel basis

deduced_from_theorem.claim: The l1 norm of a finitely supported coordinate family is nonnegative.
deduced_from_theorem.name: nonnegativity of finite l1 norm
deduced_from_theorem.description: The l1 formula is a finite sum of absolute values, hence nonnegative.

proof_steps.proof_method: By the finite l1 coordinate formula, basisNorm is a finite sum of absolute values, so it is nonnegative.

deduced_from_claim: basisNorm(v) is defined as the l1 norm of the finite coordinate coefficients of v with respect to the chosen Hamel basis

deduced_from_theorem.claim: The l1 norm of a finitely supported coordinate family is zero if and only if every coordinate is zero.
deduced_from_theorem.name: definiteness of finite l1 norm
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to the finite coordinate expansion in the Hamel basis.

proof_steps.proof_method: By definiteness of the finite l1 formula and uniqueness of Hamel-basis coordinates.

deduced_from_claim: basisNorm(v) is defined as the l1 norm of the finite coordinate coefficients of v with respect to the chosen Hamel basis

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For a scalar a and finitely supported coordinate family c, the l1 norm of a • c equals |a| times the l1 norm of c.
deduced_from_theorem.name: homogeneity of finite l1 norm
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to scalar multiplication of Hamel-basis coordinates.

proof_steps.proof_method: Scalar multiplication scales every coordinate by the scalar, and the finite l1 norm is absolutely homogeneous.

deduced_from_claim: basisNorm(v) is defined as the l1 norm of the finite coordinate coefficients of v with respect to the chosen Hamel basis

deduced_from_theorem.claim: The l1 norm of the sum of two finitely supported coordinate families is at most the sum of their l1 norms.
deduced_from_theorem.name: triangle inequality for finite l1 norm
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied coordinatewise to Hamel-basis coefficients, using the scalar absolute-value triangle inequality and summing over finite support.

proof_steps.proof_method: Coordinate addition is pointwise addition of finite coordinate families, and the finite l1 formula satisfies the triangle inequality.

proof_steps.claim: basisNorm is a norm on V.
proof_steps.proof_method: Combine the four norm axioms verified for basisNorm.

proof_steps.text: The l1 formula on finite coordinate support satisfies positivity, definiteness, homogeneity, and the triangle inequality.

proof_steps.label: homogeneous.root.cor15.proof.root.mpr.verification
proof_steps.claim: l is a homogeneous length function on G

deduced_from_claim: norm is a norm on V

deduced_from_theorem.claim: For a normed vector space, norm(v) = 0 if and only if v = 0, equivalently norm(v) > 0 for every nonzero v.
deduced_from_theorem.name: positive definiteness of a norm
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to v = g tensor 1, together with injectivity of the canonical map, to show nonzero group elements have positive length.

proof_steps.claim: For every g in G, if g != 0 then l(g) > 0.
proof_steps.proof_method: Rewrite l(g) as norm(g tensor 1). Since the canonical map G -> V is injective, g != 0 implies g tensor 1 != 0; positive definiteness of the norm gives norm(g tensor 1) > 0.

deduced_from_claim: norm is a norm on V

deduced_from_theorem.claim: In a normed vector space, norm(0) = 0.
deduced_from_theorem.name: norm of zero
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to the zero vector represented by 0 tensor 1.

proof_steps.proof_method: Rewrite l(0) as norm(0 tensor 1), identify 0 tensor 1 with 0 in V, and use norm(0)=0.

deduced_from_claim: norm is a norm on V

deduced_from_theorem.claim: In a normed vector space, norm(-v) = norm(v).
deduced_from_theorem.name: norm negation
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to v = g tensor 1, using (-g) tensor 1 = -(g tensor 1).

proof_steps.claim: For every g in G, l(-g) = l(g).
proof_steps.proof_method: Rewrite both sides using l(h)=norm(h tensor 1), use (-g) tensor 1 = -(g tensor 1), then apply norm(-v)=norm(v).

deduced_from_claim: norm is a norm on V

deduced_from_theorem.claim: In a normed vector space, norm(v + w) <= norm(v) + norm(w).
deduced_from_theorem.name: triangle inequality for a norm
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to v = g tensor 1 and w = h tensor 1, using (g+h) tensor 1 = (g tensor 1)+(h tensor 1).

proof_steps.claim: For every g h in G, l(g+h) <= l(g)+l(h).
proof_steps.proof_method: Rewrite with l(x)=norm(x tensor 1), identify (g+h) tensor 1 with (g tensor 1)+(h tensor 1), and apply the triangle inequality for norm.

deduced_from_claim: norm is a norm on V

deduced_from_theorem.claim: In a normed vector space over Q or R, norm(c • v) = |c| * norm(v) for every scalar c and vector v.
deduced_from_theorem.name: absolute homogeneity of a norm
deduced_from_theorem.description: Applied to c = n and v = g tensor 1, using (n g) tensor 1 = n • (g tensor 1).

proof_steps.claim: For every integer n and every g in G, l(n g)=|n| l(g).
proof_steps.proof_method: Rewrite with l(x)=norm(x tensor 1), use (n g) tensor 1 = n • (g tensor 1), and apply absolute homogeneity of the norm.

proof_steps.claim: l is a homogeneous length function on G.
proof_steps.proof_method: Collect the verified defining properties: positive definiteness, zero value, symmetry, subadditivity, and integer homogeneity.

proof_steps.text: Injectivity and the norm imply l(g)>0 for g != 0; also l(0)=0, l(-g)=l(g), l(g+h)<=l(g)+l(h), and l(ng)=|n|l(g).
